data.title: TSMC Q3: Strong Growth In Server AI Processor Market
data.description:
I assigned a "Buy" rating to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) in September 2024, emphasizing their 2nm and A16 roadmap. During the Q3 FY24 earnings call, the management indicated very strong demand for server AI processors, which they anticipate will account for 15% of total revenue in FY24. I reiterate a "Buy" rating with a one-year target price of US$240 per share.

TSMC's 2nm Fabrication Process for iPhone 18

As reported by the media, Apple Inc. (AAPL) is poised to make a significant change with the iPhone 18 in 2026, leveraging TSMC's 2nm fabrication process for their A20 chip. TSMC's management indicated that the mass production of their 2nm process will begin in 2025. In addition, it was reported that TSMC demonstrated its initial 2nm chips to Apple back in December 2023. Given this timeline, I think it is quite possible for Apple to adopt TSMC's cutting-edge 2nm fabrication process for the 2026 iPhone.

Apple's A20 chipset is also expected to change its packaging process from an Integrated Fan-Out to a Wafer-Level Multi-Chip Module, enabling the integration of different chips in the same package process. The design change could significantly enhance the iPhone's communication speed, delivering substantial improvement for end-users. I think it is a very positive catalyst for TSMC, showcasing their technology leadership in their 2nm process. As discussed in my previous article, the 2nm and A16 are critical components of TSMC's product roadmap, enabling the company to maintain their leadership position in the future.

Q3 and Outlook

TSMC released their Q3 result on October 17th, reporting 39% revenue growth, driven by strong performance in the high-performance computing segment, as shown in the chart below.

The strong performance was primarily driven by their 3nm and 5nm technologies, which together accounted for more than 52% of total wafer revenue during the quarter. My biggest takeaway from the quarter is the management's confidence in AI growth. As indicated over the earnings call, TSMC's management anticipates revenue from server AI processors will triple this year, reaching mid-teens as a percentage of total revenue for FY24. The strong growth in the AI processor business reflects robust demand across GPUs, AI training and inference workloads as well as other related segments such as AI servers, HBMs and custom silicon chips. Due to the strong performance, TSMC is guiding for around 33.5% revenue growth in FY24.

Valuation

Considering the strong growth YTD, I don't anticipate any earnings surprises for FY24. As such, I raised my FY24 revenue growth rate forecast to 33.5%, aligned with their guidance. For growth from FY25 onwards, I am considering the following factors:

  • I continue to anticipate the overall foundry market will grow by mid-single-digit through the cycle, primarily driven by AI chips, automotive semiconductors as well as industrial automations.
  • TSMC's 2nm and A16 will likely begin driving their revenue business from FY25. I project these new advanced processes will contribute an additional 2% growth to the overall topline. During the earnings call, the management noted that A16 is very attractive for AI server chips, with strong demand expected for both the 2nm and A16 processes.
  • TSMC has been gaining market share from Intel Corporation (INTC) and other small players. I forecast a 1% growth contribution from their market share gains.

As such, I model 8% organic revenue growth from FY25 onwards. I also revised my margin expansion to 30bps, driven by 15bps from gross profit improvement due to revenue mix towards a more advanced process, 10bps from SG&A operating leverage and 5bps from utilization improvement. As indicated in the earning call, TSMC has benefited from capacity utilization improvement in FY24. In addition, their 2nm and A16 are expected to carry a much higher gross margin once they enter the mass production phase.

The cost of equity is calculated to be 11% assuming: a risk-free rate of 3.6%; a beta of 1.16; and an equity risk premium of 6.5%. The free cash flow from equity (FCFE) is calculated as follows. Please note, the currency in the table below is New Taiwan Dollars in thousands.

Discounting all the future FCFE, the one-year target price is calculated to be US$240 per share, as per my estimates. The FX used in the model is 1 USD = 32 Taiwan Dollar.

Key Risk

On October 14th, Bloomberg reported that the US government has discussed capping sales of advanced AI chips from NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) to some countries such as China. As both Nvidia and AMD are TSMC's key customers, any potential export restrictions would pose some near-term challenges for TSMC's growth.

As discussed in my initiation report, the US government is pushing Nvidia and Apple to utilize Intel Corporation's (INTC) foundry capabilities. Currently, it is difficult to quantify the impact of these geopolitical risks on TSMC.

End Note

I am confident that TSMC's 2nm and A16 will become a significant growth driver for the company in the near future. TSMC is leading the competition in the global foundry market. I reiterate a "Buy" rating with a one-year target price of US$240 per share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: M&T Bank: Powering Higher On Strong Results
data.description:
We continue our earnings coverage of regional banks today with M&T Bank Corporation (NYSE:MTB), a larger regional firm that just reported Q3 earnings. The stock has really had a run this year, and has gone from a high-yield income name, to one that offers just 2.6% because of the sizable capital gains. We believe rate cuts will lead to the cost of funds falling while loan yields hold up over the longer term, so long as the rate cuts are slow and orderly. It is rapid large changes that really impact margins. Further, as rates abate, one would imagine loan demand picks back up, which should benefit the non-interest income sources, such as fees for mortgages and other loan originations.

We have maintained that MTB was a good long-term hold, and with valuation getting stretched here, we need to see some marked improvement in asset quality and efficiency metrics, while seeing loans and deposits grow to see more share appreciation. Let us discuss.

Earnings strong in Q3

In the quarter, the bank saw deposits dip and minimal growth in loans. That is a negative, but the earnings power was strong. Revenues were flat from last year, with M&T Bank bringing in $2.34 billion. The provision for credit losses was up to $120 million this quarter, down from $150 million last year. Non-interest income perked up to $606 million from $560 million last year. Net interest income was about flat from Q2, but return metrics improved.

M&T Bank saw net income of $721 million, or $4.02 per share. On an adjusted basis, EPS was $4.08. This was well above consensus expectations by $0.46, driven largely by lower provisions for credit losses and modest expenses.

Total deposits were $161.5 billion at the recent quarter-end, down from $163.5 billion in the sequential second quarter. Loan growth has stalled a bit. Total loans were $134.7 billion compared with $135.6 billion in Q2, so very minimal growth, but loans are up from $132.6 billion a year ago. Net interest margin improved, a key indicator, coming in at 3.62%. This was up from 3.59% in Q2, though still lower than last year's 3.79%. We expect margins to be more stable than in the past now that rates have held firm and only just started to be cut. As long as the cuts are orderly, you should not see extreme volatility in margins.

Asset quality improves

Loans are up, which is great, but we are laser-focused on asset quality in the sector. We saw provisions were down, which is a positive. We also saw loan charge-offs relax to $120 million, falling from $137 million in the linked quarter. However, this is still up from $96 million last year. Thus, net charge-offs were 0.35% in the quarter, up from 0.29% last year. We like the sequential improvement, but the elevations are a reflection of more loans and underlying weaknesses in some borrowers. However, it is well within expectations. Non-accrual has greatly improved. They totaled $1.93 billion at the end of Q3 and this is down 18% from a year ago, while dipping 5% from the linked quarter. Non-accrual loans were 1.42% of total loans. The allowance for credit losses has held firm at $2.2 billion, or 1.62% of all loans.

Now, we want to remind you that one of the reasons we have liked M&T Bank is that it is a highly efficient bank. The efficiency ratio hit 55%, which is better than we saw a few quarters ago when it was over 60%. It also improved from 55.3% in Q2. Moreover, the return metrics are strong. The return on average assets and equity have shown strength. The return on average assets improved to 1.45% from 1.31% in Q2, and is up from 1.41% in the year ago quarter. The return on average equity ticked up to 15.47% vs. 15.27% in Q2 and is up from 11.7% to start 2024. All in all, solid results.

Final thoughts

This stock remains a quality hold. The dividend is strong, though the yield has come down as investors have seen their principal shoot much higher this year. M&T Bank Corporation's Q3 was strong, with impressive earnings, improved asset quality, and increased efficiency. We see few signs in this report of trouble for the U.S. Economy or M&T Bank's customers as a whole.

data.title: CDL: A High Dividend And Low Volatility ETF, Not A Strong Growth Profile
data.description:
As interest rates decline, dividend stocks become increasingly attractive. This is a strong setup for high-yield dividend stocks and dividend-focused funds, like the VictoryShares US Large Cap High Div Volatility Wtd ETF (NASDAQ:CDL).

There is no question that CDL's dividend yield of over 3%, coupled with its low valuations, are positives from an investment perspective. On the other hand, its portfolio composition does not appear well-positioned for growth, reflecting its sector allocation and also the mature business profile of its key holdings. Thus, I see a mixed picture for this fund. While CDL makes sense as a part of a diversified income-oriented portfolio, it is not the best choice for investors seeking outperformance for the time being.

ETF Description & Highlights

CDL is an exchange-traded fund that offers exposure to U.S. large-cap companies that exhibit high dividend yields, applying a volatility-weighted methodology. It tracks the performance of the Nasdaq Victory US Large Cap High Dividend 100 Volatility Weighted Index.

This index's methodology considers the eligible universe to be the 500 largest companies in the U.S. equity market with positive earnings in the past trailing twelve months. From this group, the index provider identifies the top 100 companies by dividend yield to constitute the index. The weighting process is designed to reduce the overall volatility of the index. As a result, the selected stocks that have shown lower volatility in the past 180 days are overweight in the index, with more volatile stocks in this period being underweight. The index is reconstituted twice a year.

As of October 11, 2024, CDL had $354.7 million in assets under management, invested in a portfolio of 100 companies with an average market capitalization of $43 billion. CDL's allocation is heavily tilted towards mid-cap stocks, which represent 69.3% of the fund's assets. This mid-cap focus distinguishes CDL from both the broader market indexes, the Russell 1000 and the S&P 500, where mid-cap companies make up only 20.4% and 17.7% of the respective funds. Large caps account for 25.7% of CDL's portfolio, followed by 3.7% in mega caps and just 0.8% in small caps, in stark contrast to broader indexes, where mega caps comprise more than 40%.

CDL's top ten holdings are Kinder Morgan (1.51%), Duke Energy (1.38%), NiSource (1.35%), Williams Companies (1.34%), PPL (1.33%), Johnson & Johnson (1.33%), PepsiCo (1.30%), Philip Morris (1.29%), Southern Co (1.29%), and Entergy (1.28%). Together, these holdings comprise 13% of the portfolio, with prominent participation of utilities, with five holdings, along with players from energy, consumer staples, and healthcare sectors, which is consistent with the fund's approach, as sectors like utilities and energy are typically associated with stable cash flows and high dividend yields.

Below is a table with dividend ETFs that will serve as the peer group for this analysis. This is a blend of typical dividend funds, including the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM), a large fund with $59 billion in AUM, targeting high-dividend-paying companies, the State Street SPDR® S&P Dividend ETF (SDY), managing $21.31 billion, following the S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats Index, and Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), another heavyweight with $62 billion under management, with a focus on high-quality U.S. stocks with sustainable dividends.

The second subset of the peer group includes funds that, like CDL, take a more defensive approach by combining income and reduced risk. The first fund, Invesco S&P 500® High Dividend Low Volatility ETF (SPHD), provides an income-focused strategy with reduced volatility. Franklin U.S. Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF (LVHD) and FlexShares Quality Dividend Defensive Index Fund ETF (QDEF) also follow similar themes, with LVHD tracking the Franklin Low Volatility High Dividend Index and QDEF focusing on quality and defensiveness with the Northern Trust Quality Dividend Defensive Index.

From a sector perspective, CDL's largest allocation is to utilities, with 24.5% of total equities, followed by financial services with 19.2%, consumer staples at 17.5%, energy at 12.7%, healthcare at 6.2%, technology at 5.9%, communication services at 5.4%, consumer discretionary at 3.8%, basic materials at 3.3%, and industrials at 1.4%. Real estate has no allocation in this portfolio. This sector allocation makes CDL heavily overweight in utilities (+22.0%), energy (+9.2%), and financial services (+6.3%), three of the highest-yielding sectors, as the fund has diversified exposure to more than 30 companies in utilities and energy, while in the financial sector, it focuses on insurers and regional banks. Meanwhile, CDL is particularly underweight in technology, with allocation limited to a few companies with dividend yields above 2%, like Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO), HP Inc. (HPQ), and IBM. The same applies to CDL's underweight allocations in industrial, consumer discretionary, and healthcare, where positions are also concentrated in higher-yielding companies like Paychex, Inc. (PAYX), J&J, and Darden Restaurants, Inc. (DRI), and a few other names.

Compared to the peer group of dividend ETFs, CDL is also overweight in utilities (+12.7%), energy (+5.6%), and financial services (+7.7%), as some dividend ETFs like VYM, SCHD, and QDEF hold smaller positions in utilities and energy, while SPHD and LVHD allocate less than 10% to financials. On the flip side, CDL is underweight in several sectors, such as industrials (-7.7%), and technology (-6.1%), as all ETFs in the group have at least a high single-digit exposure to these sectors, except for low-volatility focused SPHD and LVHD. The peer group's allocation to the real estate sector is also contrasting, as most ETFs, including CDL, hold few or no positions in the sector, while SPHD and LVHD favor it, with more than 10% exposure.

CDL's dividend yield of 3.2% is marginally above the peer group, as SCHD and low-volatility ETFs (SPHD and LVHD) also offer dividend yields above 3%, reflecting SPHD and LVHD's overweight positions in utilities and real estate, as highlighted previously. Meanwhile, historical dividend growth has also been favorable for CDL, driven by dividend growth of over 10% in energy and financials.

Quite Low Valuations

CDL's average price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 13.3x represents a large discount of 47.6% relative to the Russell 1000 Index, reflecting not only its overweight allocation to low-multiple sectors, such as energy and financials, but also its stock selection process that favors lower valuations in sectors typically trading at high multiples. As a result, average valuations are relatively low in sectors such as Technology (P/E 18.6x), consumer staples (P/E 16.3x), and consumer discretionary (P/E 13.8x)

In addition, CDL's portfolio shows significantly slower growth measures relative to the Russell 1000 index, especially in the technology and consumer discretionary sectors, as evidenced by CDL's holdings such as Cisco, Corning Incorporated (GLW), and Ford Motor Company (F). The same applies to profitability metrics, as EBITDA margins dropped three percentage points for the composite portfolio, according to my estimations, driven by no holdings in large players in these sectors, particularly Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN).

Among the peer group, CDL has the lowest price/earnings ratio, reflecting its overweight allocation to the low-valuation sectors such as financials, and energy, coupled with underweight exposure to technology. The only ETFs with valuations near CDL are SCHD and SPHD, driven by SCHD's large exposure to financials and SPHD's limited position in technology.

Not A Compelling Performance, Despite Low Volatility

CDL's performance has been broadly in line with what is seen for dividend ETFs, as the fund has struggled to keep pace with the broader equity market like the Russell 1000 in positive years, but holding up better in a bearish year like 2022. Compared to the peer group, CDL outpaces those with low-volatility bias (SPHD and LVHD), while performing generally on par with typical dividend ETFs (VYM, SDY, and SCHD). From a volatility standpoint, CDL's low beta of 0.74 is consistent with its volatility-weight profile, which positions the fund, alongside LVHD, as the least volatile in the peer group.

CDL's average spread of 0.09% is not alarmingly high, but is the highest in the peer group, as dividend ETFs analyzed in this piece are much more liquid compared to CDL's low daily volume of less than $1 million. CDL's expense ratio of 0.35% is not among the cheapest ones out there, despite a relatively modest turnover of 38%. In addition, its tracking error is above the average in the peer group, while the Sharpe ratio is placed in the middle of the pack, underscored by CDL's low volatility.

Final Thoughts

In summary, CDL's low price/earnings ratio and relatively high dividend yield resonate well with its value and income-oriented profile. Furthermore, while its low historical volatility signals moderate downside risk, its sector allocation overweight in energy is seemingly a vulnerability for the portfolio. Despite the recent recovery in shares of energy stocks, the outlook for oil prices remains uncertain due to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and a lack of clarity regarding the stimulus for the Chinese economy, which is a key driver for oil demand.

On top of that, in my view, CDL's quite low exposure to technology and industrials limits its growth potential going forward. That said, although historical returns are roughly in line with the peer group of dividend ETFs, I believe that CDL is unlikely to outperform in the foreseeable future, which supports my neutral view on this fund at this moment.

data.title: AppLovin Stock Has Gone Too Far Too Fast
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

AppLovin Corporation (NASDAQ:APP) is a technology company that offers an online platform where advertisers can promote and monetize their content around the world. It operates in two main parts, Software Platform and Apps. Their offerings include AppDiscovery (for marketing), MAX (ad inventory optimization), Adjust (analytics), and Wurl (for connected TV). They also offer solutions such as SparkLabs for app store optimization and AppLovin Exchange for relocating buyers to mobile/CTV. Also, AppLovin creates free-to-play mobile games and caters to both personal as well as enterprise customers.

The APP stock has surged by 270% YTD - in less than 10 months - driven by analysts revising their revenue/EPS forecasts upward and the company showing positive developments in its fundamentals thanks to AI-related tailwinds. Despite this impressive growth, I believe the current stock price has risen too high. Previously, AppLovin failed to meet forecasts for adjusted EPS and revenue figures, yet analysts remain optimistic; so I agree with Goldman Sachs' recent downgrade, as the risk-to-reward ratio is now unfavorable for buyers at these prices.

Therefore, despite the company's strong business momentum, I decided to rate APP as a "Hold" and caution potential buyers as the stock continues to defy gravity.

Why Do I Think So?

First off, let's talk about AppLovin's recent financials (that's how I usually start my argumentation to the thesis).

AppLovin Corp posted a great fiscal Q2 2024 report with revenue totaling $1.08 billion which was 44% higher than the same period last year, largely "fueled by their software, with a 5% quarterly revenue growth, following the positive trajectory of Q1". They kept gaining from the existing operating leverage. Thus, the Adjusted EBITDA amounted to $601 million - 80% higher YoY at a 56% adjusted EBITDA margin (vs. 52% margin last quarter). The success of this campaign just proves how successful their performance platform is, which enables gaming advertisers to earn trackable revenue and profit on marketing dollars. Speaking specifically about the company's software platform, I see it drove $711 million in revenues and $520 million in adjusted EBITDA, retaining a 73% margin and growing 91% over the year ago. As the management explained, this growth is because of the latest updates in their AXON system, which makes the platform better at identifying advertisers with high-value users. Also, $369 million in revenue, up 7% from last year, with $81 million in adjusted EBITDA came from the Apps business. While revenue dropped 3% with a readjusted goal for user acquisition return, the firm has been able to reduce total app segment costs 11% quarter-on-quarter and maintain a long-term margin profile of roughly 15%.

All these results appear quite impressive, for sure. But I'm a bit puzzled by the likelihood that AppLovin may have nearly reached its peak margins, which could soon plateau - if this occurs, the company might quickly feel a cool-off in terms of its still strong operating leverage. Additionally, I'm concerned about the year-over-year decline in the absolute number of monthly active payers (MAPs), even though the average check has increased by 13%, offsetting this decline. This is troubling because losing payers could jeopardize the growth rates the company has enjoyed recently.

On the other hand, AppLovin's balance sheet continues to be solid with $446 million in free cash flow generated during Q2, a 74% flow-through from adjusted EBITDA. The company spent $356 million - employing as much as 80% of their free cash flow - to buy back its 4.2 million shares. Overall, they have repurchased and reheld 83.6 million shares over nearly $3 billion since 2022, continuing to provide shareholder value and operate with agility and liquidity. So the shareholders' returns are looking good at first glance. However, the total shareholders yield for APP (FCF plus dividends over market cap) is only 4% now, with FCF yield going under 3% because of the rise in APP's market cap as of late. But that would lead us to the valuation, which is what I'd like to discuss a bit later.

Anyway, the management appears to be quite confident about the future as the CEO Adam Foroughi predicts "a long-term 20%- 30% software business growth." AppLovin is going to focus on 3 areas of capital allocation, with emphasis on organic growth programs (e.g., engineering and business development to support AXON technology and e-commerce growth); continuing share buyback activities; and building out their balance sheet. The executives anticipate revenue of $1.115-1.135 billion ($1.125 in the mid-range), adjusted EBITDA of ~$640 million, and 57% adjusted EBITDA margin for the upcoming Q3 FY2024 (set to see the light on November 6, 2024, based on Seeking Alpha data).

Here I'd like to highlight an interesting aspect: While AppLovin exceeded the Q2 consensus EPS estimates based on GAAP, it failed to do so on an adjusted basis in the second quarter and also fell short in terms of revenue. Despite these setbacks, the management's positive outlook and the increase in margins amid rapid revenue growth have maintained a positive sentiment among Wall Street analysts. In fact, all 12 analysts have raised their forecasts for the next quarter, following the updated guidance from the management.

Another interesting point is the fundamental growth in both margins and absolute values of sales and profit for the last quarter, combined with the positive sentiment from Wall Street analysts and their unanimous belief that the company will continue to develop actively, has led to the stock nearly doubling since the publication of the Q2 report:

So this has inevitably influenced the premium that the market now assigns to the company for its past success. For FY2024, AppLovin's EPS is expected to grow by about 250% year-on-year, which is substantial. However, in FY2025, the stock is projected to trade at 32 times its earnings, which is anticipated to grow by about 29.1% YoY that year. While this represents a more moderate growth rate, these figures are still impressive, and to assess whether the current valuation of the company is fair, we can try to estimate if the growth in EPS in FY2025 aligns with where the multiple should logically be.

I acknowledge that my valuation analysis isn't perfect. However, if we consider Peter Lynch's fair value formula, which was developed many years ago to approximate value growth companies, we can use it to estimate a fair price for AppLovin stock today. So by multiplying the 2025 EPS by the growth rate, we should arrive at a fair value. Let's assume the growth rate and EPS for FY2024 are accurate, and also assume that in FY2025, the firm will exceed current forecasts by 5%. This would mean its EPS growth rate would be 35.6% YoY, rather than the 29.1% the market currently expects. Using this approach, I calculate a fair value of ~$165.6/share, which is about 15% higher than the current stock price.

It's important to note that I'm assuming quite optimistic forecasts and applying a premium to the current consensus, especially considering that the company missed analysts' estimates for adjusted earnings per share for the second quarter. A 5% premium is more of a bullish scenario than a base-case scenario. If I go with the current consensus estimates, and they're indeed accurate, then the fair value of the stock should be around $129 per share according to my calculations. This in turn is around 10.5% below the current share price.

By the way, even the model from Morningstar Premium (proprietary source) suggests a similar fair value for the APP stock today:

Therefore, considering all of the above, I conclude that AppLovin deserves more a "Hold" rating rather than a "Buy", despite its obvious growth in operating business and the clear advantages of developing its platforms.

The Bottom Line

In my opinion, the company's business is developing very rapidly, and its progress would be the envy of many similar tech companies out there. However, I believe that the adjusted EBITDA margin is approaching its peak. While I can't say exactly where this peak will be, 56% is already impressive, and it will be a challenge for the company to grow this metric further. As a result, operating leverage should begin to cool and net earnings per share growth is likely to slow. Although AppLovin's revenues are expected to increase due to the high demand for its offerings, the possible stagnation in earnings growth casts doubt on the current market's sentiment.

Today I have presented two ways to estimate the fair value of the APP stock. In one scenario, the model assumes that the company will outperform current forecasts by 5%. However, given the company's track record, it is uncertain whether this will be the case. If it does, I forecast that the stock would be undervalued by around 15%. A more realistic scenario, however, is if the current consensus forecast comes true. In this case, the stock is already overvalued by around 10-11% to its current value. Therefore, I recommend that investors either wait for the stock to fall before opening a position or, if they have already bought, hold the stock and hope that nothing negative happens when the company presents its third quarter results in early November.

I rate APP stock as a "Hold" today.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Adobe's Innovative Push Into AI Secures Long-Term Growth
data.description:
Thesis

This analysis highlights how Adobe Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:ADBE) has driven its success, especially in stock performance and its move into AI-powered creative tools like Firefly, through nonstop innovation in its Creative Cloud and Digital Media segments.

All of this, of course, is thanks to Adobe’s relentless tinkering and toying in its Creative Cloud and Digital Media segments. But, there have been a few potholes—like the cost of AI development and the inevitable slow-downs in growth in some spots. Despite these hiccups, Adobe’s clever strategy of weaving AI into its products and attracting more users seems to have them nicely set up for a good, long run.

Creative Cloud and Leadership

The software industry giant Adobe Inc. began operations in 1982 when computers were a fairly new subject for the masses. Over the years, Adobe quietly but steadily grew by snapping up companies like Macromedia, which gave the world tools like Flash and Dreamweaver—names that would soon become part of the digital landscape. This proved to be a smart move, helping Adobe assemble an impressive, all-in-one suite of software solutions. Later on, Adobe eventually dove into digital marketing with its Adobe Experience Cloud, managing to add even more to its already overflowing portfolio.

These days, Adobe is practically synonymous with its Creative Cloud suite, the go-to toolbox for just about anyone in the world of design or multimedia with heavy hitters like Photoshop, Illustrator, Premiere Pro, and After Effects.

This dominance in the creative space didn’t happen accidentally, of course. Much of it comes down to some smart leadership choices. Since CEO Shantanu Narayen took the helm in 2007, Adobe has made a bold move to shift everything to a cloud-based subscription model—a decision that’s paid off handsomely. This shift's been a big factor in why Adobe’s stock has been on such a winning streak recently.

Between December 2016 and October 2024, ADBE pulled off a stock market performance that could only be described as spectacular, with an annual return of 22.81%. To put that in perspective, the broader market, represented by the SPDR (SPY), posted a still respectable but comparatively modest 13.18% annual return over the same stretch.

In real terms, if you had the foresight to drop $10,000 into Adobe back in 2016, you’d be sitting on $50,390 today. By comparison, the same investment in the SPY would’ve grown to $26,510, which is nice, but it’s not Adobe nice.

Adobe MAX 2024 and Firefly

Adobe’s stock performance has been largely fueled by the company’s unstoppable drive for innovation, especially these days in the world of artificial intelligence. Nowhere was this clearer than at Adobe MAX 2024, where the company rolled out its vision for the future of creative tools.

Front and center was Adobe’s Firefly platform, a generative AI system that’s seamlessly woven into its apps, allowing users to create images, edit visuals, apply effects, and generate multimedia content just by typing in a few words. The whole thing is designed to supercharge workflows for creatives, and so far, it’s been a massive hit. In fact, users have already churned out more than 12 billion Firefly-powered creations using AI-enhanced tools like Photoshop, Illustrator, and Premiere Pro.

Another perfect, corporate, real-world example of Adobe’s innovative muscle comes from Red Hat (an IBM company), which saw a tenfold boost in time savings after introducing Adobe Express into its global marketing operations.

Strong Q3 Financials

This burst of innovation was mirrored quite nicely in Adobe’s fiscal Q3 earnings, where the company reported a tidy $5.41 billion in revenue, up 11% from the previous year. The Digital Media segment led the way, bringing in $4 billion, with Creative Cloud and Document Cloud driving the growth.

But the real star was Document Cloud, posting an 18% increase and hitting $807 million. Demand for PDF and Acrobat solutions is only growing stronger. Both have been supercharged by AI tools like Firefly, proving that even the humble PDF can benefit from a little futuristic magic.

Freemium Models and Growth

This impressive performance wasn't limited to Document Cloud, as Adobe's Adobe's Creative Cloud remains strong, pulling in $3.19 billion in revenue with an 11% year-over-year growth. A big driver behind this is the company's focus on AI innovations and seamless integration across its cloud platforms. They're also seeing growth thanks to their push for new user acquisition. They’ve cleverly introduced freemium models—think Firefly—which make it wonderfully easy to reel in fresh faces and turn them into loyal customers.

E-Commerce and Mobile Insights

Beyond the innovation — Adobe's been taking a big leap into e-commerce, and Adobe Analytics and Experience Cloud are contributing to this. For instance, they expect $240.8 billion in online sales in the US for the holiday season 2024 — this is just one of the ways their analytics pick up the pulse of the market.

But it's not only about predicting big numbers; it’s also about how Adobe leverages those insights for business. With Adobe Analytics, companies get a direct feed of what’s happening with their customers, what’s failing, and how to change that moment-to-moment.

This results in more relevant marketing, personalized shopping, and higher conversion rates. That’s directly linked to sales growth for Adobe’s customers and is an indication of how entrenched Adobe is in the online marketplace. Experience Cloud takes it even further by making all of these insights accessible in one unified system, so companies can optimize everything from recommendations to mobile engagement.

Such efficiency is critical to firms wanting to remain in the game. Adobe is, then, more than providing tools; it's delivering results that help improve both the performance of clients and its profitability.

Risks & Headwinds

Firefly's fast growth, showcased at Adobe MAX and in the Q3 earnings, underscores Adobe's leadership in AI-driven creativity. But with that growth come some hurdles, especially around handling AI-related costs.

API (Application Programming Interface) calls have tripled quarter-over-quarter, showing just how critical Firefly has become in expanding Adobe's creative platform. At the same time, the earnings report pointed out concerns over the rising costs tied to AI development. As investments in AI training ramp up, there's a risk those expenses could squeeze profit margins if revenue growth doesn't keep up.

Furthermore, the high cost of AI video production tools, for example, could put a damper on future profitability, especially with competitors in the AI space evolving fast. Adobe is still figuring out pricing models for AI tools like Firefly, and they’ll need to strike the right balance between making money and getting users on board.

And lastly, Adobe's latest quarterly results revealed that Creative Cloud isn't growing quite as fast as its sibling, Document Cloud. Now, this could be a sign that the creative tools market is maturing — at 11% growth, Creative Cloud seems to be inching toward middle age, possibly nudged along by the increasing swarm of competitors in the AI-driven creativity space. Broader economic issues and potential belt-tightening among big corporations could also stir up trouble, especially when a sizable chunk of Adobe's income comes from heavy hitters like Amazon, Meta, and Disney.

Q4 Outlook, Valuation and Investment Rating

With these obstacles in mind, ADBE’s blended P/E ratio of 27.92x may seem high, but that's quite typical in the tech sector. Additionally, ADBE's 22.76% growth in adjusted operating earnings shows its ability to expand, driven by the products I just discussed.

This growth, in turn, supports its current valuation, especially when compared to the average P/E ratio of 39.68x, which actually suggests there may be more room for growth if performance remains strong. In short, the slight 5% dip in stock price over the past month doesn't signal more concerning issues. With solid earnings growth and a manageable P/E ratio, Adobe seems appropriately priced for growth-focused investors.

Given these strong fundamentals and manageable valuation, the focus now shifts to how Adobe will continue driving performance. As the company heads into Q4 with revenue projected between $5.50 billion and $5.55 billion, the real question is how well they can keep balancing innovation with profitability.

Their leadership in AI-powered creative tools, like Firefly, is undeniable, and they’re killing it in the Digital Media and Document Cloud segments, further proving their innovation-first strategy. With double-digit revenue growth and AI integrations making people more productive, it looks like Adobe’s setting itself up for continued long-term success. Moreover, they're buying back shares and generating solid cash flow, key signs of a company that’s got its act together. I’d call Adobe a “Buy.”

data.title: StandardAero: Good Company, But Wait For A Better Entry Point
data.description:
Initiating Coverage

I am initiating coverage on StandardAero (NYSE:SARO) with a 'Hold' rating. StandardAero’s initial public offering was priced at $24 and the stock has witnessed a strong listing and trades at $31.7. I believe that StandardAero is an attractive investment from a long-term perspective. I would however wait for a better entry point after the initial exuberance cools off.

As an overview, StandardAero identifies itself as the “world’s largest independent, pure-play provider of aerospace engine aftermarket services for fixed and rotary wing aircraft.” The end market for the company includes commercial, business aviation, and military.

In the engine aftermarket value chain, StandardAero is a provider of maintenance, repair and overhaul, engine component repair, on-wing, and field service support. It’s worth noting that for the year ended December 2023, 77% of the company’s revenue was from long-term contractual agreements.

For FY 2023, StandardAero reported revenue of $4.6 billion, which was 10% higher on a year-on-year basis. For the same period, the company’s adjusted EBITDA was $561.1 million. With this overview, let’s discuss the fundamental factors that makes StandardAero stock worth considering on declines.

Key Investment Positives

Well Diversified End Market – If we consider the company’s revenue by end market, the business is well diversified. As of FY 2023, StandardAero derived 54% revenue from the commercial aerospace segment. Further, 21% of revenue was from business aviation and another 21% from military & helicopter. It’s worth noting that across these segments, the company’s customers include some of the top OEMs. This provides earnings stability as these customers have continued need for MRO services.

Relatively High Entry Barriers – As of FY 2023, 89% of the company’s revenue was from engine services with 11% from component repair services. Engine services is a highly specialized area that requires extensive approvals and certifications from government regulators and OEMs. The company has license and authorization for MRO on 40 key engine platforms. The company’s clients include GE Aerospace, CFM International, Pratt & Whitney, Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY), Honeywell (HON) and Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF). I therefore believe that StandardAero will maintain leadership position with continued investment in new engine platform programs.

Emerging Growth Catalysts - In March 2023, the company became the first and only independent aftermarket service provider in North America to join the authorized service network for the LEAP-1A and LEAP-1B engines. These are expected to account for over 35% of the world’s installed base of engines by 2033 and will significantly contribute to growth. Further, the company has expanded its capacity in the CFM56 platform. It’s the largest engine platform in the world with more than 33000 engines delivered to date, powering more than 600 operators. The addressable market is therefore significant and the company is ahead of the curve when it comes to ramping-up its capability to service the latest and widely used engines. It’s also worth noting that since 2017, the company has completed 11 acquisitions. This strengthens the company’s position in a fragmented industry.

EBITDA Margin Expansion – For the year ended December 2021, Standard Aero reported adjusted EBITDA margin of 10.9%. For the last financial year, EBITDA margin expanded by 140 basis points to 12.3%. Further, for the first half of 2024, the company’s adjusted EBITDA margin was 13%. With steady growth likely to sustain, I expect continued EBITDA margin expansion driven by operating leverage. Further, the component repair service segment was 10% of the revenue in FY 2021. In the last financial year, this segment contributed to 11.2% of the revenue. For the first half of 2024, engine services segment had an adjusted EBITDA margin of 13%. On the other hand, the component repair segment reported an adjusted EBITDA margin of 26%. If the revenue contribution from component repair services continues to increase, it will have an incremental impact on the EBITDA margin. As EBITDA swells, it’s likely to support improvement in credit metrics as StandardAero has significant debt.

Global Diversification – Between 2021 and 2023, StandardAero reported revenue growth of 27% from the United States to $2.9 billion. For the same period, international revenue increased by 40% to $1.7 billion. Clearly, geographic diversification has supported the company’s growth and as the total addressable market expands, Standard Aero is positioned to benefit. Overall, the company estimates that the aerospace aftermarket was is worth $250 billion for 2024. Therefore, there is ample headroom for growth and the contribution of international markets as a percentage of total revenue is likely to continue increasing.

Besides these factors, StandardAero reported a total liquidity buffer of $516.4 million. This provides ample flexibility for potential acquisitions or investment in new or expanded platforms. A recent example being the new LEAP platform and the expansion of CFM56 platform.

Key Risk Factors

As of June 2024, StandardAero reported total debt (including current portion) of $3.3 billion. Further, for the first half of 2024, the company’s adjusted EBITDA was $336 million. Considering an annual EBITDA of $672 million, StandardAero has a leverage of 4.9x. Leverage is therefore high and has impacted the bottom-line.

Having said that, there are two important points to note –

First, Standard Aero reported interest expense of $155.6 million for the first half of 2024. Considering an annualized interest expense of $311 million and an annualized adjusted EBITDA of $672 million, the EBITDA-interest-coverage comes to 2.16x. Debt servicing is unlikely to be a concern considering the point that revenue and EBITDA is likely to increase at a steady rate.

Second, the company estimates that the IPO proceeds will be approximately $1.2 billion. The total debt is likely to decline to $2.1 billion and will translate into improvement in credit metrics. Assuming no further debt, leverage is likely to decline to 3.1x by the end of 2024.

I must add here that StandardAero reported operating cash flow of $27.3 million and $67.9 million for the last two financial years. OCF has been relatively depressed, but I expect improvement in the coming quarters as leverage declines.

Overall, I rate the financial risk as “medium.” However, the company’s business risk is low because entry barriers are high. Further, StandardAero has been investing in business transformation that delivers growth. LEAP and CFM being the recent examples. This will ensure that the company’s addressable market remains significant and recurring revenue continues to swell.

It’s worth noting that the company’s military and helicopter segment is relatively immune to economic shocks. However, the commercial and business aviation segment contributes to 75% of the revenue and growth is likely to be impacted by macroeconomic headwinds.

For the medium-term, this risk is mitigated by the point that the world is moving towards relatively low interest rates. It’s likely that expansionary monetary policies will support GDP growth acceleration in 2026 and beyond.

Concluding Views

Since 2016, StandardAero has been awarded licenses and authorizations for eight unique engine programs. This underscores the company’s operational and execution capabilities coupled with the credibility with OEMs.

Currently, StandardAero commands a market valuation of $10.8 billion. Further, if we consider the pro-forma debt of $2.1 billion, the current enterprise value is $12.9 billion. For the year, it’s likely that StandardAero will deliver adjusted EBITDA of $672 million (1H 2024 annualized). Therefore, the EV/EBITDA comes to 19.2.

StandardAero does not have direct peers. However, we can compare the valuations with sector median for the aerospace and defense industry. The forward EV/EBITDA (sector median) is 12. Even if we consider the possibility of relatively higher top-line growth for StandardAero, valuations look stretched. It therefore makes sense to consider exposure to the stock on corrections.

It’s worth noting that for FY2023, StandardAero reported year-on-year revenue growth of 10%. For the first half of 2024, the company’s growth was 12% on a year-on-year basis. With the catalysts discussed, I believe that top-line growth is likely to remain healthy. The geopolitical tensions factor will have a positive impact on the military and helicopter segment.

With the potential decline in leverage, StandardAero will have higher financial flexibility. This is critical as the company has continued to pursue opportunistic acquisitions. Overall, StandardAero stock is worth considering on correction. As the global economy grows, the aviation industry will expand and so will the need for MRO services.

data.title: Lithium News: Arcadium Lithium, Q2 Metals
data.description:
After launching Nuclear News last week, Green Growth Giants [GGG] now brings Lithium News. This is an opportune time too, with big M&A developments bringing the otherwise beaten down sector back in focus and as a fifth of the over 50 lithium stocks tracked by GGG saw double-digit increases in the past week. This edition looks at the key movers and shakers in the sector in the past week.

Big Lithium Newsmakers

#1. Arcadium Lithium: Acquisition target

Note: Since the article was first published exclusively on GGG on October 7, Arcadium Lithium has been acquired by Rio Tinto in an all-cash deal for a sum of USD 6.7 billion.

Australian multicommodity miner Rio Tinto (RIO), the third-biggest by market capitalisation in the world after BHP (BHP) and China Shenhua Energy (OTCPK:CSUAY), is looking to acquire lithium miners.

The iron ore focused company, which also produces aluminium and copper, has a minuscule presence in lithium mining so far. But it has bigger ambitions in the segment, as it confirmed reports of approaching Arcadium Lithium (NYSE:ALTM) in a press release earlier today.

What does the deal mean for Rio Tinto?

If the acquisition bid for the Ireland headquartered, vertically integrated lithium producer with global interests goes through, Rio Tinto would become the third-biggest lithium miner in the world after Albemarle (ALB) and SQM (SQM).

At the same time, it would still be a small acquisition for the mining behemoth, adding only 1.6% to its total revenues, as indicated by figures for both companies for 2023. Though, going by the proposed valuation (as discussed below), it can impact the company's financials (depending on how the deal is structured), at close to 70% of its trailing twelve months [TTM] free cash flow and 46.5% of the TTM net income.

What's next for ALTM shareholders?

According to Reuters, ALTM is expected to be valued at between USD 4-6 billion or more. After is 10% rise in a single day following initial news of the takeover, the company's enterprise valuation is at USD 4.4 billion based on its TTM sales.

If the valuation lands at the midpoint of the speculated range, there's a relatively limited sub-13% price increase due. But if it's closer to USD 6 billion, ALTM will see a 35.3% upside. Either way, though, the stock is a speculative trading play right now.

In any case, analysts have positive expectations for Arcadium Lithium's earnings per share [EPS] (see table below). Its non-GAAP forward EPS at 16x is already lower than the 16.75x for the materials sector, and going forward it only gets better. So even if the acquisition doesn't work out, there's still upside ahead for the stock. The bottom line here is, that no matter how we look at it, ALTM is a good buy.

#2. Q2 Metals: Price doubles in a month

The Canadian junior lithium miner Q2 Metals (OTCQB:QUEXF) (QTWO:CA) had a great past week, with a 45% increase, the biggest among lithium stocks tracked by GGG. This continues the stock's upswing which has doubled in the past month and is up by 337% year-to-date [YTD] (see chart below).

Why did the stock rise?

Its stock price rally followed positive results from the latest drilling results at its Cisco Project in Quebec. Speaking of these results, Neil McCallum, Vice President Exploration of the Company said “These assays continue to validate the potential and scale of the Cisco Property as that of a larger mineralized system”, while Alicia Milne, President and Chief Executive Officer said “We are extremely pleased with these assay results, which continue to demonstrate the world-class nature of the Cisco Project.”

Can it continue to rise?

The company is still at early stages, with no economic evaluation available on the project so far. Its liquidity levels indicate a need to raise more funding in the future, with its working capital-to-operating expenses ratio of 2.45x, reflecting that it has only two and a half years of spending worth of liquidity available. The company successfully did a private placement worth USD 7.5 million in July, and it has no debt on its books either, so funding should not be a problem.

But the stock price has risen quite a bit already and its TTM price-to-book (P/B) ratio at 3.9x is far higher than the 1.8x for the materials sector, which makes it less attractive. This is especially so as there can be risks ahead that come with early-stage mining operations.

Other notable stocks

  • Piedmont Lithium (PLL): The US-based company, which is developing integrated lithium operations, saw a price jump on being granted an environmental permit for its Ewoyaa Lithium Project in Ghana. It was up by over 30% in the past week and has made over 50% gains in the past month.
  • Albemarle (ALB):The leading lithium producer is in the progress of restructuring in a bid to drive efficiencies, including cost savings, with management changes. The company reported a gross loss in two of the past four quarters, and its price is down by 29% YTD, even after it has made gains in the past month.
  • Ioneer (IONR): The Canadian miner is one of the few lithium companies to see a YTD uptick in price as it inches closer to production at its Rhyolite Ridge lithium-boron site in Nevada. However, the past week saw a pullback of 13% for the stock, likely on profit taking after rising on receipt of environmental clearance earlier in September.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Crown Castle Can See Growth In The Easing Economic Environment (Upgrade)
data.description:
Crown Castle Inc. (NYSE:CCI) is undergoing a major restructuring and is positioning its operations for brownfield growth in its small cell & fiber business. The firm’s plan includes a cut of 7,000 greenfield small cell nodes that will save the firm an estimated $800mm in capital investments through 2026. With the refocus towards less risky projects, I have reason to believe the majority of the cutbacks are behind Crown Castle and that the firm is positioned to return to growth in the coming year. Given the firm’s current valuation, site decline going into Q4 ’24, and the modest growth estimate going into FY25, I am upgrading my rating from a SELL to a HOLD with a price target of $116/share at 16.6x eFY25 price/AFFO.

Crown Castle Operations

Crown Castle is working rigorously to right-size the firm with a 10% reduction of workforce to meet its operational capabilities. As announced in June 2024, the firm is undergoing a major restructuring as well as a review of its fiber business. As of Q3 ’24, management believes that the fiber assets are in optimal strategic locations with the necessary capacity to support both the existing and growing demand from wireless & broadband customers.

The focus of the fiber and small cell business is to build upon the firm’s existing colocations and reduce its greenfield exposure. The prioritization of its colocation strategy is estimated to deliver 2% revenue growth for FY24, down from the previous outlook of 3%. One of the major demand drivers for fiber is AI network connectivity. This factor could be a major turning point for Crown Castle in the coming years, assuming the firm retains the fiber business, as the data center footprint is estimated to grow by a CAGR of 23% through 2030.

Despite the significant growth for data centers, the biggest limiting factor to achieving this growth will be sourcing baseload capacity, which may impact the locations of these AI factories. Amazon (AMZN) announced that it is partnering with utilities to deploy small modular reactors (“SMRs”) to provide the necessary baseload capacity for its AI factories. Though this is good news for the industry, the SMRs will not be operational until 2030-2035 as the technology is still under development.

In 3’24, Crown Castle mutually agreed to cancel 7,000 contracted greenfield small cell nodes, up from 3,000-5,000 announced in June, that were previously accounted for in its backlog. These small cell nodes would have required more than $800mm in capital investments that would have been deployed in eFY25-26 with below-average yields. These contract cuts were worked out with the firm’s customers, as the small cell nodes had a higher cost than anticipated and would have resulted in a negative impact to both Crown Castle’s and its customers’ expected returns.

Net of the canceled contracted nodes, Crown Castle has 40,000 small cell nodes in its backlog. Management expects that this backlog will result in double-digit organic revenue growth going forward. Over 70% of the backlog are colocation node and are expected to have returns higher than the firm’s existing footprint.

Management is expecting to add a total of 11,000-13,000 small cell nodes in eFY24, up from 8,000 nodes in FY23.

Crown Castle Financial Position

Management updated their forecast for the duration of eFY24 with some minor shifts in site rental revenue and net income. Site rental is expected to come in at $6,340mm, down -3% from FY23 revenue of $6,532mm, and net income is expected to come in at $1,020mm, down -12% from the previous estimate of $1,158mm. Net income is now estimated to decline by -32% rather than -23% as part of the previous estimate. The lower net income outlook reflects the impact of a $125-150mm asset write-off related to reductions in its small cell business.

Crown Castle raised $1.25b through the issuance of 5-year & 10-year senior unsecured notes at 5.1%. Accordingly, proceeds were used to pay down the firm’s commercial paper program. With $371mm in cash & $24b in total debt, Crown Castle has a net debt/aEBITDA ratio of 5.69x.

Looking ahead to eFY24, I’m forecasting Crown Castle to generate $6,551mm in net revenue with an AFFO/share of $7.01/share. I’m forecasting modest growth in eFY25 as the firm continues its strategy across small cell and fiber projects. I’m forecasting revenue to grow to $6,692mm and an AFFO/share of $7.05/share.

Comparing my forecast with my outlook back in June 2024, I believe Crown Castle will still be navigating choppy waters as the firm finds its grounding. There are some exogenous factors that will put Crown Castle in a better position. The Fed’s recent 50bps rate cut and the potential for an additional 25-50bps cut in November can help improve Crown Castle’s financial position. $500mm of 1.350% senior unsecured notes will roll off the balance sheet, if the firm elects to refinance this relatively small maturity. The macroeconomic outlook is also less dismal, with inflation falling to 2.4%. The GDP growth outlook has also significantly improved, with q2’24 printing 3% growth.

Risks Related To Crown Castle

Bull Case

There is a growing demand for fiber in the US that is driven by higher compute needs as AI training and inferencing is leveraged both at cloud and private data centers. Further densification of towers will support growth for Crown Castle, especially as edge AI capabilities are deployed across mobile and other endpoint devices. The current macroeconomic environment is also improving, providing support for REITs in general. Further interest rate cuts will likely improve the appeal for REITs, including CCI shares.

Bear Case

The capabilities of LEO communications satellites are improving and may be expanded in the coming years to support the mobile broadband coverage in the US. With firms like AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) deploying test satellites in coordination with AT&T (T) and Verizon (VZ), satellites may be leveraged as an alternative to terrestrial towers.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

CCI shares currently trade at around the midpoint of its historical trading range at 16.57x TTM price/AFFO. Considering Crown Castle Inc.’s restructuring initiative and refocus of its small cell & fiber business, I have reason to believe the majority of the pain is behind the firm, allowing for growth going forward.

Considering the price action post-Q3 ’24 earnings, I have reason to believe investors will be seeking a more solidified answer to the fiber business. This segment is a high margin business that will be the solution necessary to support the higher compute demands for AI applications.

In terms of dividend safety, Crown Castle should be able to cover the payout in eFY25; however, the firm will likely need to refinance its 2026 debt to cover the dividend in full. The biggest risk posed here is that Crown Castle may be refinancing at a higher cost of debt if the Fed slows its pace of rate cuts.

data.title: Kontoor Brands: No Longer A Good Fit
data.description:
Even though I have never been much of a fan of the clothing and apparel market, I have always been a sucker for high quality companies that are trading on the cheap. One such example of this can be seen by looking at Kontoor Brands, Inc. (NYSE:KTB), which owns both Wrangler and Lee jeans. If you have ever heard of jeans, you have certainly heard of both of these brands. Even though the company has suffered from lumpy financial performance over the last couple of years, the stock was cheap enough to warrant my attention. I regret not buying shares. But I don't regret rating it a 'buy'.

Since I last reaffirmed the company as a 'buy' candidate last October, shares have skyrocketed by 82.5%. This dwarfs the 38.4% increase seen by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. But that pales in comparison to when I initially rated it a 'buy' in March 2022. From that time through today, shares are up 125.2%. The S&P 500, meanwhile, is up by only 38.4%. This assessment of mine was based on how attractively priced the shares were. But unfortunately, all good things must come to an end. While the company has proven successful in returning its bottom line to growth, revenue has shown weakness for a while now. Furthermore, shares are no longer as cheap as they were. To me, they fit the definition of 'fair value'. And in fact, relative to similar companies, they might even be considered a bit pricey.

Ultimately, this is leading me to downgrade the company to a 'hold'. But this doesn't mean that my decision will be this way forever. As new data comes in, my opinion could very well change. And it just so happens that the next batch of data, covering the third quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year, should come out before the market opens on October 31st. If things go really well, I could upgrade the company once again. But I think that is unlikely.

Time for a downgrade

Fundamentally speaking, things have been rather mixed for Kontoor Brands as of late. Consider, for instance, how the company performed during 2023 compared to 2022. Year over year, revenue actually declined by 0.9% from $2.63 billion to $2.61 billion. Management attributed this to a 1% decline in US Wholesale revenue and a 5% drop in non-US Wholesale revenue. The decline in the US was because of lower wholesale shipments caused by retailers more tightly managing their inventory levels following a normalization after the COVID-19 pandemic came to an end. Outside of the US, the company suffered from reduced demand in the Asia Pacific region, as well as weakness in its digital wholesale operations in the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) operating regions.

This is not to say that everything was bad. The firm did actually experience growth in its US digital wholesale business. But unfortunately, it was not enough to offset the weakness elsewhere. Interestingly, revenue would have been even lower in 2023 had it not been for a $5 million benefit that the company enjoyed involving foreign currency fluctuations. On a brand-by-brand basis, all the weaknesses of the company actually came from its Lee line of products. Sales dropped from $874.4 million to $842.5 million. However, Wrangler did reasonably well, with sales inching up by $8.3 million. However, rounding to the nearest $10 million would still give us a year-over-year figure of $1.75 billion.

With revenue dropping, profits and cash flows for the company also suffered. Net income declined from $245.5 million to $231 million. In addition to suffering from a decline in sales, the company also saw a contraction in its gross profit margin totaling 1.4%. This was because of a 3.7% contraction caused by higher inventory expenses as inflationary pressures impacted not only the cost of products, but also various input costs. Things would have been worse had it not been for the benefit of pricing adjustments and other factors that added 1.9% to the company's gross profit margin and had it not been for a 1.1% benefit resulting from a reduction in the firm's use of air freight. Other profitability metrics also declined year over year. Operating cash flow surged from $83.6 million to $356.5 million. But once we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a decline from $304.5 million to $286.6 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company contracted from $402.5 million to $360.9 million.

Mixed financial results for Kontoor Brands continued into the 2024 fiscal year. However, I would say that the picture is definitely better than the 2022 to 2023 window was. Revenue did unfortunately fall from $1.28 billion to $1.24 billion for the first half of each respective year. Management chalked this up mostly to inventory actions being taken by its retail customers, though a decline in sales associated with seasonal products also had an impact. However, even as revenue dropped, profits for the company improved rather nicely.

For the first half of this year, net income for the business came in at $111.3 million. That's an increase of 8.4% compared to the $102.7 million the company reported one year earlier. This was in spite of the fact that the company suffered from higher selling, general, and administrative costs that came from increased incentive-based compensation and greater investments in the firm's direct-to-consumer operations. The improvement, according to management, came from the cost of goods sold, with the company's gross profit margin expanding by 310 basis points because of a 440 basis point benefit caused by lower product costs and a reduction in downtime from the firm's own internal manufacturing. Other profitability metrics also improved. Operating cash flow grew from $111.7 million to $152.7 million. On an adjusted basis, it went from $136.8 million to $148.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company inched up from $180 million to $186.1 million.

When it comes to the rest of this year, management seems to have mixed expectations as well. For 2024 and its entirety, they are forecasting revenue of between $2.57 billion and $2.63 billion. This would be roughly flat at the midpoint compared to what the company saw last year. Earnings per share should now come in at around $4.80. That's at the very high end of the previously expected range of $4.70 to $4.80 that management revealed earlier in the year. Should this come to fruition, it would translate to a net income of about $271 million. This compares favorably to the $231 million the company reported for 2023. They are also forecasting an operating cash flow of $350 million. That's down from $356.5 million last year. However, it's up from the prior expected guidance of $335 million.

For the purpose of valuing Kontoor Brands, we are stuck with a bit of a difficult situation. My own preference is to use adjusted operating cash flow instead of operating cash flow. I believe this gives a better picture of the operational health of the company. I could assume that adjusted operating cash flow would drop at the same rate that operating cash flow should. However, for the first half of this year, adjusted operating cash flow is up 8.6% compared to the same time last year. After careful consideration, I decided to give the company the benefit of the doubt and use the higher figure of $350 million compared to the $311.3 million I calculated we would get by annualizing the adjusted operating cash flow figure. But even using this, we can see that shares are pricey on a relative basis. So at the end of the day, even giving the business the benefit of the doubt doesn't change my overall stance. Lastly, if we do annualize EBITDA, we would get a reading of $373.1 million for the year.

Using these estimates for 2024, and historical results for last year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. If the business was growing at a rapid pace, I could justify a 'buy' rating at these prices. But that's not the case here. To me, this signals a company that is very solidly fairly valued on an absolute basis. But then, in the table below, I compared the firm to five similar enterprises. On a price-to-earnings basis, two of the five companies ended up being cheaper than our candidate. However, this number increases to four of the five when using the other two profitability metrics.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Kontoor Brands 16.8 13.0 13.6
Columbia Sportswear Company (COLM) 21.3 6.5 9.4
Capri Holdings Limited (CPRI) 25.5 14.4 13.3
PVH Corp. (PVH) 7.7 5.7 5.5
Under Armour, Inc. (UA) 12.1 6.9 41.6
Gildan Activewear Inc. (GIL) 19.5 12.4 12.9

As I stated earlier in this article, my opinion could change as new data comes in. And before the market opens on October 31st, management will be providing results for the third quarter of this year. Analysts are currently forecasting revenue of $663.1 million. This would be a bit surprising since it would imply an increase over the $654.5 million reported for 2023. Then again, if Kontoor Brands is to see revenue remain roughly flat year over year, it only stands to reason that the second half of this year would show higher year-over-year figures.

On the bottom line, analysts are forecasting earnings per share of $1.24. This would be up from the $1.05 the company reported for the third quarter of 2023. This would translate to an increase in net income from $59.5 million to $70 million. Unfortunately, estimates have not been provided when it came to other profitability metrics. But in the table above, you can see what these were for the third quarter of last year. I would wager that if net profits increase as analysts expect they will, that cash flows will also improve as a result.

Takeaway

All things considered, I really like Kontoor Brands. But this doesn't mean that I think the company is worth buying. In the past, its low trading multiple was enough to appeal to me, even though the business was suffering from mixed financial results. But today, shares look fairly valued, and I see no reason to believe that the stock will continue going up. Due to these factors, heading into the earnings release, I have decided to downgrade it to a 'hold'.

data.title: Top Dividend Funds: JEPQ Vs. QQA For Monthly Income Up To 10%
data.description:
Two great monthly payers

As I start to transition into a full-time writer and an individual partially living off of my investments, finding stable ways to beat money market funds and still maintain reliable liquidity has been top of mind. The recent trends in hybrid index funds that have both a stock segment [in larger proportionality] and an options segment have become very popular, especially as rates on money markets and high-yield accounts begin to trend downward.

This has also been a 3-month investigation into figuring out how all of these funds work, as they are all a little bit different. I've made calls to JPMorgan and Invesco and really appreciate all the help they have provided me with to understand these products. Today I will compare two excellent funds, the Invesco QQQ Income Advantage ETF (NASDAQ:QQA) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ).

In my opinion, both are great options to maintain exposure to the market and have a source of liquidity that shouldn't jump all over the place when you need to sell shares to pay expenses. These are all the things I'm looking for, and the strategy has begun to make more sense the more I study these two sets of funds.

I also want to emphasize that this is not a strategy I'm taking for my portfolio broadly. I have stocks and ETFs that I hold and plan to hold for as long as possible. To me, these funds are where I would like to start moving some of my money market positions to, positions that I would use to supplement income through share sales.

Here are some of the differences between these two monthly payers

Here is a bullet point list of the differences and similarities between these two funds:

  • QQA is managed by Invesco [the creator of The Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) and The Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP)].
  • JEPQ uses a proprietary stock-picking strategy to emulate the Nasdaq 100 and attempt to provide alpha to the strategy.
  • QQA uses QQQ as an index to replicate the index fund exactly for the stock proportion of this fund. This is a true index fund hybrid versus a beta plus index emulator.
  • JEPQ writes covered calls for the income portion of the portfolio against Nasdaq futures, the strategy ranges between 15-20% of the overall portfolio.
  • QQA writes calls and puts for the income portion of the portfolio against Nasdaq futures and has a lower exposure at around 5-10% weight.
  • JEPQ has a TTM dividend of 9.51%
  • QQA is expected to have a forward dividend yield of around 10% according to Invesco and Bank of America Global Research.
  • Both payout the dividends and premiums as the total payout representative of the dividend paid by the fund.
  • JEPQ has an expense ratio of .35%
  • QQA has an expense ratio of .29% with a current special promotion: "Effective July 17, 2024 through December 31, 2024, Invesco Capital Management LLC (the “Adviser”) will voluntarily waive 100% of its management fee, 0.29%, for the Fund. The Net Expense Ratio for the Fund through December 31, 2024 is 0.00%."

Similarities

  • Both are run by very large institutions that have well-established derivatives trading desks.
  • Both utilize the ELN format for the options slice of the portfolio that provides income.
  • Both pay monthly dividends.

Why ELNs matter

This is the portion of the equation that had me up at night contemplating how it all worked. I had read the prospectus of both and listened to various interviews with Hamilton Reiner, but I still couldn't quite figure out all the moving parts.

After having a lengthy call with Invesco and chatting with JPMorgan a few times, I'm confident I have the crux of the equation, and it makes enough sense to me to be a viable strategy:

So, in order to avoid risking any of the actual equities in the portfolio being called away, the strategies simply take a proportion of the funds and fully fund their index futures option position with a counterparty. These funds are now encapsulated in note form, and any income from the buying or selling of options with the counterparty will be treated as income versus a return of capital.

From my understanding, these ELNs both buy an index future from an established counterparty and then write a call option against them [in most instances]. This is usually done monthly to match the monthly income. The counterparty would make some money on the spread for the sale of the index future, and both the buy/writer and the counterparty get a benefit.

Eventually, this fully funded position of trading with the counterparty would mature or end, after which I assume the funds are then reinvested into a similar setup again and again. The strategy would be ratcheted up or down depending on where the portfolio manager sees the options market going.

Why big institutions matter

There are many options overlay strategies out there, but only a select few can actually utilize the ELN format to where the manager does not have to risk the underlying stocks being called away. In many of the smaller option/stock ETFs we see in the market, NAV erosion is much more prevalent as actual stock positions are getting called away to provide the yield to the investor.

Oftentimes, the best options premiums are on the stocks with the highest momentum, and the manager who cannot utilize an ELN format may have to risk the stocks with the best momentum or RSI in order to keep up the yield.

Only institutions with established derivatives trading desks could easily set this up, and I would only recommend the funds that are run by large institutions currently for that reason.

Holdings comparisons

JEPQ Top 10

Ticker Security Description
(AAPL) APPLE INC COMMON STOCK
(NVDA) NVIDIA CORP COMMON STOCK
(MSFT) MICROSOFT CORP COMMON
(META) META PLATFORMS INC
(AMZN) AMAZON.COM INC COMMON
(GOOG) ALPHABET INC COMMON
(AVGO) BROADCOM INC COMMON
(TSLA) TESLA INC COMMON STOCK
(NFLX) NETFLIX INC COMMON STOCK
ELN NDX_19

As we can see with JEPQ, the top 10 holdings are a bit different than the Nasdaq 100 which we can see below with QQA and QQQ. With the higher weighting of options in the JEPQ portfolio, one of the ELN option notes also makes its way into the top 10 holdings.

This portfolio does have more active management and turnover than QQA which simply seeks to match the QQQ index fund.

QQA Top 10

Compared to QQQ

So as we can see, QQA is a true index fund replicator, as the top 10 of QQA are exactly the same as QQQ. There are some different holdings proportionality due to the cash and ELN positions, but the strategy is the same with no turnover unless the index changes.

Why monthly matters

My strategy with these funds is not to use the income as it rolls in, but rather reinvest it to smooth out the principal balance. As the funds hold stocks, they also move with the market, although their beta should be much lower than the market in general.

Monthly reinvestments will keep the return trajectory line smooth. Then depending on market conditions, bull or bear, I would simply sell LIFO [last in first out] or FIFO [first in first out]. In a bull, I would be selling last in first out as my older shares probably have more capital gains, and in the case of a bear market, sell first in first out assuming that the earliest sets of shares have some tax loss harvesting.

With monthly reinvesting, this selling of shares strategy should work out overall, as selecting individual share tranches by date usually has a tranche limitation. This is how I intend to operate this strategy for liquidity once I start moving some money market funds to utilize tax efficiency.

Examples of muted volatility

Here is a 3 portfolio example over the course of 1 year.

  • Portfolio 1 : JEPI 50% JEPQ 50%
  • Portfolio 2: JEPQ 100%
  • Portfolio 3: JEPI 100%

In this 1 year period, all three scenarios have a max drawdown that is around 50% less than the S&P 500 (SPY) or The Nasdaq (QQQ). During this same period of Jan 1 2023-Sep 30th 2024, the S&P 500 had a max drawdown of -8.32% and QQQ had a max drawdown of -8.42%.

Total returns all the while have been very comparable to their index targets. Furthermore, the monthly reinvesting versus quarterly quickly lowers your cost basis during drawdown periods.

The examples above are based on JPMorgan's products, but I expect the Invesco products to hold up as well.

Why own multiple?

My opinion is, owning a few different covered call overlay funds diversifies risk as it relates to the options trading strategies. As ELN agreements are bespoke to the negotiations between the two parties involved, these are not items that are disclosed and will perform better or worse depending on the talent of the manager.

Knowing how this works better, I now would only want to be involved in the index fund option overlay products that have institutions behind them that are large enough to execute the ELN structure. My hang-up on these was the NAV erosion possibility due to writing options against actual owned shares, which then creates further reinvestment risk after the manager gets back a chunk of cash for a stock that is now more expensive to go and repurchase in the market.

Other options

Other great products from the two companies that I would intend to cover later would be their more value-oriented products, namely The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and The Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Income Advantage ETF (RSPA). With JEPI more or less indexing the low-vol S&P 500 index and RSPA mimicking the S&P 500 equal eight, those are actually very attractive to me right now with lower overall P/E ratios for the underlying holdings.

Both of these again sell options within the ELN wrapper, which protects the stocks from being called away while still providing monthly income.

Risks

All of these strategies have risk associated with their options slice, which also entails counterparty risk. The main risks associated with this structure are laid out here by Invesco and should be consistent with all of the option overlay funds:

Investments in ELNs are susceptible to the risks of their underlying instruments, which could include management risk, market risk and, as applicable, foreign securities and currency risks. ELNs are also subject to certain debt securities risks, such as interest rate and credit risks. Should the prices of the underlying instruments move in an unexpected manner, the Fund may not achieve the anticipated benefits of an investment in an ELN, and may realize losses, which could be significant and could include the Fund’s entire principal investment. An ELN investment is also subject to counterparty risk, which is the risk that the issuer of the ELN will default or become bankrupt and the Fund may not be repaid the principal amount of, or income from, its investment. ELNs may also be less liquid than more traditional investments and the Fund may be unable to sell ELNs at a desirable time or price.

Again, this is limited to the slice that produces the income, and these funds further diversify the risk by splitting their ELN proportion amongst multiple ELNs with multiple counterparties.

Also, please note that some platforms may not allow you to trade ETFs with active management and or futures trading baked in. As of right now, Merrill Edge in particular does not allow investors to buy or sell some of these products.

Summary

For those who are looking to get more exposure to the market by redirecting capital into equities, these two funds provide a way to do that with lower volatility and not too much capped upside. Again, these products I think are excellent for a portion of your monthly expense accounts that you intend to liquidate to pay for day-to-day income needs. I still believe the majority of one's portfolio should be in long-term buy-and-hold stocks and index funds.

Both of these are run by large reputable firms that have advantages in writing options against smaller boutique firms. Both of these are a buy.
